coredata.dc:title: Tandem structured luminescent solar concentrator based on inorganic carbon quantum dots and organic dyes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Mateen, Fahad
Ali, Mumtaz
Lee, Sae Youn
Jeong, Sung Hoon
Ko, Min Jae
Hong, Sung-Kyu
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we demonstrate the simultaneous use of carbon quantum dots and organic dyes as highly emissive luminescent material in high performance tandem luminescent solar concentrator (LSC). The top LSC layer is based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), while the bottom one is based on organic dye. The role of förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is demonstrated in organic dyes in a bottom LSC. Moreover, the CQDs layer which has the capability to harvest ultraviolet (UV) and near-UV photons acts as a protective layer to improve the photo-stability of the organic dyes contained in bottom waveguide. The electrical measurements showed that the optical conversion efficiency (ηopt) and power conversion efficiency (ηPCE) of CQDs based single LSC are 5.62% and 1.03% respectively. While, ηopt, and ηPCE the dye-LSC are 13.42% and 2.72% respectively. However, in the tandem LSC, overall ηopt and ηPCE are 16.32% and 3.2% respectively. Our results showed that tandem structured CQDs and dye based luminescent solar concentrators make the practical use of LSCs more feasible because of the unique properties such as good photostability and high efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
FRET
Luminescent solar concentrator
Photostability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of inorganic CQDs and organic dyes based tandem LSC, each waveguide with the dimensions of 5 × 5 × 0.3 cm2. The incident of the incoming solar radiations is normal to the surface of the LSC. (b) Once cell configuration of solar cell in LSC (top view).
Photographs of (a) CQDs based waveguide under AM 1.5G spectrum (b) Organic dyes-based waveguide under AM 1.5G spectrum (c) Organic dyes-based waveguide under room light included with scale.
Characterization of prepared CQDs. (a) TEM images of CQDs, (b) XRD diffraction pattern of CQDs.

Normalized absorption and emission spectra of (a) CQDs for top waveguide, (b) Yellow 10GN, and Red G for bottom waveguide. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Edge emission spectra of stacked dye films and blended dye film. The blended dyes film is a single film comprising 0.15 wt% Yellow 10GN and 0.10 wt% Red G in PMMA matrix. Stacked single dye films contain single-dye films (0.15 wt% Yellow 10GN/PMMA on top, 0.10 wt% Red G/PMMA on the bottom) physically stacked on top of one another. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Emission intensity of Red G and Yellow 10GN/Red G blend at 600 nm as excitation wavelength is swept across the absorption range of Yellow 10GN and Red G. The blended film shows enhanced Red G emission response in the range of Yellow 10GN's absorption. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Edge emissions of dyes before and after UV exposure for 70 h.
Photovoltaics measurements of single and tandem LSCs.

head.ce:title: Tandem structured luminescent solar concentrator based on inorganic carbon quantum dots and organic dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Nano Engineering Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Nano Engineering Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy and Material Engineering, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy and Material Engineering, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we demonstrate the simultaneous use of carbon quantum dots and organic dyes as highly emissive luminescent material in high performance tandem luminescent solar concentrator (LSC). The top LSC layer is based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), while the bottom one is based on organic dye. The role of förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is demonstrated in organic dyes in a bottom LSC. Moreover, the CQDs layer which has the capability to harvest ultraviolet (UV) and near-UV photons acts as a protective layer to improve the photo-stability of the organic dyes contained in bottom waveguide. The electrical measurements showed that the optical conversion efficiency (ηopt) and power conversion efficiency (ηPCE) of CQDs based single LSC are 5.62% and 1.03% respectively. While, ηopt, and ηPCE the dye-LSC are 13.42% and 2.72% respectively. However, in the tandem LSC, overall ηopt and ηPCE are 16.32% and 3.2% respectively. Our results showed that tandem structured CQDs and dye based luminescent solar concentrators make the practical use of LSCs more feasible because of the unique properties such as good photostability and high efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional solar concentrators typically use a complex assembling of lenses and tracking systems to focus sunlight on a high-performance photovoltaics (PV) cell, thereby reducing the amount of required PV material. However, the added cost of an electronic tracking system contributing to installation expense to maintain high concentration and bulky configuration makes them unattractive for the use in building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV) systems (Chou et al., 2013). Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) are photon-management devices that are a cost-effective alternative to conventional sunlight concentrating systems. In addition to improving the performance of stand-alone photovoltaic cells, LSCs can be employed into the built environment, for example in the form of semi-transparent solar windows that would transform energy-passive building facades into large-area energy generation units (Kerrouche et al., 2014; Zhao and Lunt, 2013).

The concept of a LSC was initially suggested in the late 1970 s by Goetzberger and Greubel (Goetzberger and Greube, 1977) and Weber and Lambe (Weber and Lambe, 1976). Generally, LSCs consist of a transparent slab embedded in or coated with an emissive fluorophore. Direct and diffused sunlight is absorbed by the fluorophore and is re-emitted at longer wavelengths. A portion of the re-emitted light, guided by total internal reflections, propagates towards a photovoltaic cell placed at the edge of the slab, where it is converted into electricity (Currie et al., 2008; Van Sark et al., 2008). Like geometric concentrators, LSCs are also characterized by their power conversion efficiency (ηPCE), which is the ratio of the electrical power energy to the incident radiative energy. Moreover, a true concentration strength of LSC is described by the concentration factor C, which is the power generated from solar cells attaching to the LSC relative to the bare solar cell. Since being introduced, LSCs have been struggling with the decreased ηPCE and C due to various deficiencies such as re-absorption losses due to overlapping of absorption and emission bands (Giebink et al., 2011; Peeters, 2011; Wilson et al., 2010), escape cone losses (Debije et al., 2008; MacQueen et al., 2010; Slooff et al., 2008a; Yamada et al., 2010), limited absorption spectrum (Bailey et al., 2007; Carlotti et al., 2016; Sanguineti et al., 2012) and decreased photo stability of fluorophore (Griffini et al., 2013; Hyldahl et al., 2009; Kinderman et al., 2007; Reda, 2007). Besides exploring new design such as stacked structures (Carlotti et al., 2016; Earp et al., 2004; Goldschmidt et al., 2009) and fiber LSCs (Banaei and Abouraddy, 2013; Correia et al., 2015; Edelenbosch et al., 2013), major research efforts to cope with the given challenges were focused on the employment of suitable fluorophores. For example, (Slooff et al., 2008b) presented a stacked structured LSC contained with preylene based organic dye as fluorophore and the obtained power conversion efficiency (PCE) was 7.1%, which is still the highest efficiency ever reported for LSC device. Later on, other organic dyes such as rhodamines (Verbunt et al., 2009) and coumarines (El-Bashir et al., 2014; Mateen et al., 2017) have been investigated for LSC applications. Dye alignment systems (Mulder et al., 2010a; Mulder et al., 2010b; Tummeltshammer et al., 2014) and förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between organic dyes (Banal et al., 2014; Davis et al., 2017) has also been discussed to improve the performance of dye based LSCs. However, low photo stability and narrow absorption spectrum still remain the important issues for most of the LSC-suitable dyes.

Quite recently, semiconducting inorganic quantum dots (QDs) have been employed as the potential candidate for LSC application due to their enhanced photo stability, broad absorption spectrum and tunable emission controlled by their size. To date, various types of QDs have been used in LSC e.g. CdSe/ZnS (Sholin et al., 2007), CdSe/CdZnS (Li et al., 2016), PbS (Shcherbatyuk et al., 2010), Mn doped ZnSe/ZnS (Erickson et al., 2014), PbS/CdS (Zhou et al., 2016), CuInS(Se)2 (Meinardi et al., 2015), CuInS2/CdS (Knowles et al., 2015), CuInS2/ZnS (Li et al., 2015), and indirect band gap Si QDs (Meinardi et al., 2017). Recently, Yunxia Li et al. (Li et al., 2017), for the first time, presented the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based LSC. CQDs belongs to novel class of non-toxic semiconducting materials and their synthesis involves a simple solvothermal approach which is extremely cost competitive (Baker and Baker, 2010). So far, the reports on CQDs based LSC are few (Gong et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018). There still exist a huge potential to improve the performance of LSC using CQDs.

In this study, we demonstrate a tandem LSC (5 × 5 × 0.3 cm2) based on inorganic CQDs and organic dyes. The proposed design of tandem LSC consists of two waveguide LSC separated by a 100 µm air gap. The top waveguide is based on CQDs, while the bottom one is based on organic dyes as shown in Fig. 1 . Such design improves the overall efficiency of LSC device. Moreover, the top CQDs waveguide which has the capability to harvest ultraviolet (UV) and near-UV photons and acts as a UV protective layer to improve the photo-stability of the organic dyes contained in bottom waveguide. To prepare the top waveguide, CQDs/polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) thin film was coated onto high optical quality glass. While the bottom waveguide was constructed by incorporating two organic dyes (Macrolex Yellow 10GN and Macrolex Red G) into the bulk polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) plate. Macrolex Yellow 10GN and Macrolex Red G show photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) of 87% an 86% respectively (El-Shaarawy et al., 2007; Friedman and Parent, 1987), and are connected in FRET network, such that Yellow 10GN acts as donor and Red G acts as acceptor. FRET is a non-covalent and non-radiative excitation energy transfer that uses two or more dyes which absorb light over a wide range of wavelengths but most of the emission of the LSC originates from the dye that emits at the longest wavelength. Thus, FRET approach in our design ensures the reduction in reabsorption losses while increment in the absorption range. Moreover, our results show that CQDs not only contribute to enhance the PCE of tandem LSC, but also improve the photo stability of the organic dyes connected through FRET network.

In this study, citric acid, ethylenediamine (EDA), ethanol, de-ionized water, methyl methacrylate (MMA), and 2,2′-Azobis 2-methylpropionitrile (AIBN) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The fluorophore used for bottom waveguide were organic dyes Macrolex Yellow 10GN and Macrolex Red G and were supplied by Bayer, Germany. All the chemicals were used without further purifications. Titanium dioxide coated white diffused reflector was purchased from local vendor.

CQDs were synthesized according to the method mentioned in the literature with slight modifications (Qu et al., 2016). In explanation, 1.5 g citric acid and 3 g urea were dissolved in 15 mL dimethylformamide. After stirring, the mixture was transferred into an autoclave and reacted for 6 h at 160 °C. The mixture was then added dropwise to 60 mL hexane to precipitate the CQDs. The precipitates were collected and dispersed in 60 mL ethanol. For the Na + treatment, the solution was mixed with 20 mL NaOH aqueous solution, and stirred for 5 min. The mixture was then added dropwise into hexane to precipitate the CQDs. The precipitates were dispersed in 60 mL ethanol. The prepared solution was moved into dialysis bags with a molecular weight of 3000 Da for one day. The CQDs/ethanol solution inside the dialysis bag was collected by opening the dialysis bag and pouring the solution into a separate tube. The reported quantum yield of prepared CQDs is 46%.

To make top waveguide part of tandem LSC, doctor-blade deposition method was employed (Li et al., 2016). 15% PVP solution was prepared in ethanol. And then 2.5% of synthesized CQDs were added in that polymer solution and vigorously stirred at 800 RPM for 1 h. The CQDs/PVP mixture was poured onto optically clear glass substrate (5 × 5 cm2) to form a strip in front of a coating rod. The rod was swiftly translated over the glass, leaving behind a viscous CQDs/PVP layer, which turned into a highly uniform film after evaporation of ethanol. The glass substrate used in the study had a thickness of 0.3 cm and the QD/polymer total film thickness was about 50 µm. To prepare bottom waveguide, 0.1 wt% AIBN, 0.015 wt% Yellow 10GN and 0.010 wt% Red G were mixed with MMA and sonicated for 15 min. To start the reaction, this blend solution was heated at 85 °C. After 30 to 45 min, the blended products turned into a syrupy liquid indicating the formation of pre-polymer. The thick syrup was then cooled to room temperature and poured into the glass mold. The resulting product was dried in the mold at 45 °C for 24 h. Finally, the polymer plate was cut into square pieces (5 × 5 cm2) and polished to obtain the 0.3 cm thick bottom waveguide. To construct tandem LSC, the individual waveguides were combined while maintaining an air gap of 100 µm between them. White diffuse reflector was also used on the backside to avoid back surface losses. Fig. 2 shows the photographs of CQDs and organic dyes-based waveguide LSCs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM JEOL JEM 2100F) was used to measure the size distribution of CQDs. Wide angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku D/MAX-2500) having the Cu Kα radiation was used to study the graphitic planes of CQDs. Absorbance and fluorescence spectra of CQDs, Yellow 10GN and Red G were measured using UV-Visible (UV-Vis) spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary Eclipse) respectively.

High-efficiency (20%) crystalline silicon (c-Si) solar cells purchased from PuguangSolar China were used for photovoltaics measurements of tandem LSC. Electrical contacts were soldered to the front and rear side of the solar cells. Optically transparent refractive index matching epoxy adhesive (United Adhesives, OE 1582) was used to bind the solar cells with LSC. The edge emission spectrum of blended dyes and current-voltage measurements of tandem LSC were taken by illuminating the sample surface with a solar simulator (Mc-Science) having Xenon arc lamp of 160 W, as a light source equipped with filters to approximate Air Mass (AM) 1.5 global spectrum. (Percent non-uniformity of irradiance; ≤±2%, Spectral match; 0.75–1.25, Temporal instability; <2%). The light intensity was calibrated with the KIER calibrated Si-solar cell, before performing the efficiency test and found to be 100 mW/cm2.

The CQDs were obtained according to the experimental procedure described in the previous section. To obtain the information about the size and shape of the CQDs, TEM technique was employed. A well dispersion and circular morphology of CQDs can be observed in the TEM images (Fig. 3 (a)). A magnified TEM image clearly shows the circular morphology and average size of CQDs (5.5 nm). The XRD pattern of CQDs powder illustrated in Fig. 3(b) shows a broad peak centered at 21°- 22° which refers to 002 graphitic planes, with a calculated interlayer spacing 0.39 nm – 0.42 nm (±0.04). The broadness of XRD peak is due to the amorphous nature and small size of synthesized quantum dots (Gao et al., 2015). Additionally, the increased interlayer spacing as compared to natural graphite (0.335 nm) is because of less effective π-π stacking interaction owing to the rich intrastriatal defects and the presence of excessive functional groups on the surface of CQDs (Hu et al., 2014; Sahu et al., 2012).

Normalized absorption and emission spectra of CQDs in PVP matrix are shown in Fig. 4 (a). The absorption spectrum of CQDs is characterized by the absorption over the entire UV spectrum and a part of visible spectrum. The broad absorption spectrum spans from 350 with the tail that extends up to 700 nm. This broad UV and near UV absorption is attributed to π–π* transition of the CC bond while the extended tail is because of n–π * transition that appears due the presence of amide and other functional groups on their surface (Biroju et al., 2015; Rajender and Giri, 2016). Moreover, the emission spectrum of CQDs is also shown in Fig. 4(a), which spans from 500 nm to 700 nm with the maximum peak at 565 nm. The Stokes shift, defined by the difference between absorption and emission peaks, is one of the important factors that influence the performance of LSCs. Small stokes shift as a result of overlapped absorption and emission spectra causes the reabsorption losses (Qu et al., 2016). In this case, there is an overlapping of absorption and emission spectrum of CQDs, however, the overlapping is not significantly large. Fig. 4(b) shows the individual absorption and emission spectra of Yellow 10GN and Red G in chloroform. In case of Yellow 10GN, absorption and emission peaks are found at 460 nm and 512 nm respectively. Red G with relatively wide absorption spectrum gives the absorption maximum at 535 nm and emission maximum at 591 nm. These spectra indicate that significant increment in the absorption of the solar spectrum (range 400–600 nm) can be achieved when both dyes are used together. Moreover, the emitted light that is not trapped inside the CQDs based waveguide can be reabsorbed by the bottom organic dyes based waveguide, thereby allowing more light to be concentrated on the edges of LSC.

To obtain the edge emission spectrum of Yellow 10GN/Red G blend in polymer matrix, we prepared a single film on microscopic glass slide, with PMMA as the host matrix polymer. Additionally, separate Yellow 10GN and Red G films were also prepared on two distinct glass slides and stacked together such that the Yellow10GN film was above the Red G. Edge emission spectra (Fig. 5 ) of a single film (containing a blend of both dyes) and stacked dye films were taken using integrated sphere method as described in experimental section. The spectral sensitivity of commercially available c-Si solar cell is also plotted along with the emission spectra. It is worth to note that the maximum overlap had occurred between the spectral response of c-Si solar cell and the edge emission spectra of blended dyes film and stacked dye films; however, c-Si solar cell performs better at higher wavelengths as shown in Fig. 5. Further, it can be seen from Fig. 5 that the edge emission spectrum of the stacked dye films under given light source closely matches their independent superposition (see Fig. 4(b)), while the emission spectrum of blended dye film is entirely the same as that of Red G. The results can be explained on the base of fӧrster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from Yellow 10GN to Red G. FRET is a non-covalent and non-radiative excitation energy transfer, and is observed when there is a an appreciable overlap of emission spectrum of donor (Yellow 10GN) with the absorption spectrum of acceptor (Red G) (Masters, 2008). Yellow 10GN upon excitation; transfer its energy to Red G through highly efficient FRET process and does not show its own emission band. An important description to evaluate the FRET process is fӧrster critical radius (Ro) which is regarded as a distance between donor and acceptor at which 50% of energy could transfer efficiently and lies in the range from 10 to 100 Å (Sapsford et al., 2006). The value of Ro can be defined as the Eq. (1) (Joseph and Lakowicz, 1999).
(1)
R o
= 0.211
[
κ
2
n
- 4
Q D
J
λ
]
1 / 6
where, κ2 is the dipole orientation factor, which is considered as 2/3 when the molecules of donor and acceptor are randomly distributed in polymer matrix (Bailey et al., 2007), QD is the quantum yield of the donor, n is the refractive index of host medium and J(λ) is the spectral overlap of acceptor’s absorption and donor’s fluorescence emission. In the case of Red G/Yellow10GN blend mixture, the Ro was found to be 23 Å which is quite less than previously reported DCJTB/DPATPAN (53 Å) (Banal et al., 2014) and LR305/DCM energy transfer pair (39 Å) (Balaban et al., 2014).

Additionally, the FRET in Red G/Yellow10GN pair was evaluated by measuring the emission peak (600 nm) at the varied excitation wavelength. It can be seen clearly from Fig. 6 that in LSC, where Yellow 10G was not included, the Red G peak emission intensity was enhanced by increasing the excitation wavelength till 520 nm. Further increase in the excitation wavelength had a negative effect with reduced emission intensity. On the other hand, for LSC having Yellow 10G, the maximum excitation wavelength was found to be 460 nm due to the addition of Yellow 10GN. Moreover, LSC with the blend of Red G and Yellow10GN showed an enhanced Red G emission, even though the 460 nm excitation used to excite the LSC is primarily absorbed by Yellow 10GN.

PV behavior of LSC system is generally used to evaluate its performance. In this study, CQDs and FRET connected organic dyes (blended mixture of Yellow 10GN/Red G) based waveguides, each having the dimension of 5 × 5 × 0.3 cm3, are prepared according to the method elaborated in experimental section and stacked together to form a tandem LSC. Before presenting PV response of our device, we first introduce the definition of some concept terms widely accepted in LSC research. Optical conversion efficiency (ηopt) is described as the ratio of LSC edge emission power to the absorbed incident power. And power conversion efficiency (ηPCE) of LSC is defined same as for solar cells, which is the ratio of the electrical power output to the solar power input. It is important to mention that a higher ηopt and ηPCE are not the only critical criteria for LSC performance evaluation. For the practical implementation of LSC, it should deliver more power than the bare solar cell; otherwise it will not fulfill its purpose of concentration. Therefore, the actual concentration strength is given by the optical concentration factor C* which is defined as the ratio of the electrical power delivered by the solar cell attached to the LSC edge to solar power incident on the entire LSC surface area. The formulas used to calculate ηopt, ηPCE, and C* are given as following (Banal et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2015);
(2)
η
o p t
=
I
S C
×
A
E d g e s
I
S o l a r c e l l
×
A
L S C
(3)
η
P C E
=
J
S C
×
V
O C
× F F
P
I N
(4)
C
⁎
=
η
o p t
×
A
t o p
A
e d g e s
where ISC is the short-circuit current measured from the solar cell attached with the edge of LSC and Isolar cell is the short circuit current measured from the same solar cell under direct illumination of the same light source. Atop and Aedges are the LSC’s top surface area and the area of its lateral faces where the solar cells are attached. Ratio Atop/Aedge is generally termed as geometric gain (G). JSC is the short circuit current density, Voc is the open circuit voltage, FF is the fill factor from soar cell attached to LSC edge, and Pincident is the incident solar power density on the top of the LSC (mW/cm−2).

Photovoltaic performance of our tandem LSC is evaluated under one cell configuration (Fig. 1(b)) and the values of photovoltaic parameters and LSC efficiencies including ηopt, ηPCE and C* are also listed in Table 1 . According to the results, ηopt, ηPCE and C* of CQDs based single LSC are 5.62%, 1.03% and 0.87 respectively. While, ηopt, ηPCE and C* of the dye based single LSC are 13.42%, 2.72% and 2.22 respectively which are quite comparable to the other dye based LSC systems (Krumer et al., 2017; Li et al., 2016). In the tandem LSC, the first layer LSC based on CQDs shows ηopt and ηPCE of 4.34% and 0.85%. Likewise, the second layer of LSC based on dyes shows ηopt and ηPCE (11.97% and 2.35%) less than that of single LSC, resulting in the overall ηopt and ηPCE 16.32% and 3.2% respectively. Further, the C* of tandem LSC is 1.35 which shows that the tandem LSC delivers more power than the bare solar cell, hence, fulfilling its purpose of concentration. The enhanced efficiencies observed in CQDs based single LSC could be the result of light scattering effect offered by the bottom diffused reflector. In contrast, the top CQDs layer of LSC in tandem structure has organic dye based LSC instead of diffuse reflector on its rear side. Regarding bottom FRET connected dye based LSC, the relatively reduced efficiencies after placing the CQDs LSC on its top is due to the decrease of absorption of sunlight as in this system, there is a little overlap of the absorption between CQDs and organic dyes. Additionally, the first LSC based on CQDs also reflect some of the incoming light resulting in decreased absorption in bottom LSC. Further, it is important to mention that ηPCE of our CQDs and FRET connected dyes based tandem LSC is significantly higher than the recently presented CQDs based tandem LSC, for example, 1.1% ηPCE of UV and visible CQDs based tandem LSC (Zhou et al., 2018), 1.4% ηPCE of CQDs and CdSe/CdS QDs based tandem LSC (Liu et al., 2018), and 3.05% ηPCE of CQDs and perovskite QDs based tandem LSC (Zhao et al., 2018). Furthermore, ηPCE of CQDs based single LSC is also higher than that of the previous reports. The observed increment in the ηopt and ηPCE in the tandem LSC over single LSCs is attributed to the facts that tandem structures have much stronger overall absorption across the solar spectrum (350–600 nm) compared with a single layer LSC. Also, the light that is lost from the bottom surface of top CQDs LSC can be reabsorbed by the bottom organic dyes LSC.

Stability of LSC is one of the key features that need to be considered for its successful utilization. Generally, the instability of dyes under UV radiations potentially limits their long-term use in LSC. In this study, we additionally analyzed the photostability of our device by illuminating it with UV light. The results indicate that after UV exposure for 70 h, the edge emission of dye based LSC without the presence of top layer (CQDs LSC) is 41% less than the original dye LSC before UV illumination. In contrast to this, dye based LSC in the presence of top layer showed a 27% decrease in edge emission after 70 h UV exposure (see Fig. 7 ). The results are explained by the decreased photo-oxidation of dyes in the presence of top CQDs layer. Further strategies to overcome the stability issue is the polymer surface passivation of CQDs as the extremely reactive and vulnerable surface groups of CQDs are shielded under the polymer layer. In addition, surface passivation protects and enhances the photoluminescence features of CQDs, leading to much higher quantum yields values and thus photoluminescence intensities.

In this study, we proposed a high-efficiency tandem LSCs based on carbon quantum dots and FRET connected organic dyes. Prepared CQDs showed the brilliant optical properties allowing them to be utilized for efficient emitters for LSC. The as-fabricated semi-transparent tandem LSCs showed an optical and power efficiencies of 16.32% and 3.2% respectively under sunlight illumination (100 mW/cm2) which represents a significant enhancement in efficiency compared with single LSCs. Moreover, we successfully demonstrated that the existence of CQDs in the top layer significantly improves the long-term photostability of dye based LSC which is due to the excellent photostability of CQDs and its strong absorption of UV light, which decreases the UV intensity in the dye based LSC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. 2019R1A2C1005805), Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (20194030202320) and (2018201010636A).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2019.08.045.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bailey et al., 2007

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optimized excitation energy transfer in a three-dye luminescent solar concentrator

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bailey, S.T., Lokey, G.E., Hanes, M.S., Shearer, J.D., McLafferty, J.B., Beaumont, G.T., Baseler, T.T., Layhue, J.M., Broussard, D.R., Zhang, Y.-Z., 2007. Optimized excitation energy transfer in a three-dye luminescent solar concentrator. Solar energy materials and solar cells 91(1), 67-75.
